content:
5 min read
Just now

--

For a long time, I misunderstood what programming was actually useful for.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by RONIT on UnsplashI thought coding meant building apps.
Websites.
Games.
Complex software.
Big technical projects.
And while all of that is true…
I had completely overlooked something surprisingly powerful.
Programming can quietly remove work from your daily life.
Not glamorous work.
Not impressive engineering.
Just small repetitive tasks that slowly consume time every single day.
And strangely enough…
That realization changed how I think about Python completely.
Because one small automation script taught me something I had never fully understood before.
Sometimes writing a few lines of code feels less like programming… and more like hiring an invisible assistant.
And once I experienced that feeling for the first time…
I started seeing automation everywhere.
I Had A Task I Kept Repeating Every Single Day
At the time, I had a painfully repetitive workflow.
Nothing difficult.
Actually…
The exact opposite.
The task was extremely simple.
Which somehow made it even more frustrating.
Every day, I needed information from several websites.
Mostly small updates.
Product prices.
Availability changes.
Research information.
Occasionally, article content.
The routine never changed.
Open browser tabs.
Search for updated information.
Copy the data manually.
Paste it into a spreadsheet.
Repeat again tomorrow.
And repeat again the next day.
At first, I barely noticed how annoying this process had become.
Because individually, each task felt small.
That assumption turned out to be expensive.
I Had Quietly Accepted A Bad Workflow
One afternoon, I started estimating how much time I had been wasting.
The number surprised me immediately.
Twenty minutes every day.
Over two hours every week.
Nearly ten hours every month.
All spent doing work that required almost no thinking.
No creativity.
No decision making.
No judgment.
I was simply moving information from websites into spreadsheets.
And suddenly something obvious hit me.
Why am I doing work a computer could probably do automatically?
That question stayed in my head.
And eventually pushed me toward building something useful.
I Decided To Automate The Entire Process
At that point, I had already spent months learning Python.
Variables felt comfortable.
Loops made sense.
Functions no longer confused me.
I had built small beginner projects.
But most of them felt disconnected from reality.
Calculator apps.
Guessing games.
Tutorial exercises.
Interesting…
But not genuinely useful.
This problem felt different.
For the first time, I wanted Python to solve a real problem I personally cared about.
So naturally…
I decided to automate the workflow.
The Script Looked Surprisingly Small
I expected automation to feel complicated.
Instead, the first version looked surprisingly manageable.
Something like this: import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup response = requests.get(url)
Then, parsing the website.soup = BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser")
Extracting the information.
data = soup.find("h1")Repeating across multiple pages.
for url in urls:
scrape_page(url)Saving everything automatically.
df.to_csv("results.csv")I remember staring at the script thinking:
“That’s actually much simpler than I expected.”
Sometimes, difficult-looking projects become surprisingly manageable once you start building.
Watching The Script Work Felt Strange
I still remember the first successful run.
Normally, I would spend twenty or thirty minutes manually collecting information.
This time…
I pressed run.
And simply watched.
The script opened websites automatically.
Collected the information.
Organized the data.
Saved everything neatly.
No browser tabs.
No copy-pasting.
No spreadsheets.
No repetitive work.
And honestly…
The experience felt weird.
Not because the code was impressive.
But because it felt like something was quietly working on my behalf.
That feeling stayed with me.
It Felt Like Delegating Work To Something Invisible
A strange thought hit me after watching the script run.
This no longer felt like software.
It felt like delegation.
I wasn’t personally doing the work anymore.
Something else was doing it for me.
Quietly.
Consistently.
Without mistakes.
Without boredom.
Without effort.
And suddenly I understood something fascinating.
Automation feels remarkably similar to hiring an assistant.
Except the assistant never sleeps.
Never complains.
Never forgets.
And works the same way every single time.
That realization completely changed how I think about programming.
I Started Seeing Invisible Assistants Everywhere
After this project, I noticed repetitive work constantly.
Checking websites manually.
Downloading recurring reports.
Updating spreadsheets.
Renaming files individually.
Tracking job listings.
Monitoring product prices.
Collecting research data.
Moving information between applications.
And now every repetitive task triggered the same question.
“Can Python quietly handle this instead?”
Because once you experience code doing work for you…
You stop viewing programming the same way.
That mindset shift changed everything.
The Script Quietly Taught Me Multiple Skills
The funny part is, I thought I was building one simple automation project.
But the script forced me to strengthen multiple Python concepts simultaneously.
HTTP requests.
requests.get()HTML parsing.
BeautifulSoup()Loops.
for page in pages:File handling.
to_csv()Error handling.
try:
...
except:
...Debugging failed requests.
Understanding website structure.
Learning how automation systems behave.
One small project unexpectedly taught much more than tutorials had.
And real-world projects often do exactly that.
I Finally Understood Why Developers Love Automation
Before this project, I misunderstood automation completely.
I assumed automation meant advanced engineering.
Massive backend systems.
Complex software infrastructure.
Fancy technical work.
This project taught me something much simpler.
Automation means removing unnecessary work from your own life.
That’s all.
And once you experience that personally…
Programming stops feeling educational.
It starts feeling powerful.
That distinction changed everything for me.
The Biggest Lesson Had Nothing To Do With Code
Looking back, the biggest lesson surprised me.
Programming isn’t valuable because code looks impressive.
Programming is valuable because it creates leverage.
A few minutes spent writing code can permanently remove hundreds of hours of future work.
That leverage compounds enormously over time.
Before this project, Python felt theoretical.
Interesting.
Useful.
But abstract.
After this project…
Python felt completely different.
It felt like a tool capable of quietly working on my behalf every single day.
And that realization permanently changed how I think about learning code.
The Rule I Follow Today
Whenever I catch myself repeating the same digital task multiple times, I immediately stop and ask:
“Can Python quietly do this for me instead?”
If the answer is yes…
I stop doing it manually.
Even if automation requires effort upfront.
Because work delegated once often stays delegated forever.
And that leverage compounds dramatically over time.
Final Thoughts
Looking back, this small automation project taught me one of the most valuable lessons I’ve learned while programming.
Sometimes code doesn’t feel like software.
Sometimes code feels like delegation.
That repetitive task had quietly consumed time every single day.
I had normalized it.
Accepted it.
Stopped questioning it.
Then one small Python script permanently removed it from my life.
And suddenly something interesting happened.
It no longer felt like I was doing the work.
It felt like something invisible was quietly doing the work for me.
And honestly…
That feeling completely changed how I see programming.
Before this project, coding felt like learning technical skills.
After this project…
Coding felt like hiring leverage.
A way to build invisible assistants that quietly remove unnecessary work from everyday life.
And that realization is exactly why I keep learning Python.
